timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1-13 — "Cursed Is the One Who Trusts in Man"

heb.paragraph: Judah's sin is "engraved (ḥārût) with an iron stylus, with a diamond point" on the tablet of their hearts (v.1). The metaphor inverts the tablets of the law — instead of God's commandments inscribed on stone, sin is inscribed on hearts. What was meant to receive Torah has received transgression. This anticipates the new covenant promise (31:33) where God will write his law on hearts, overwriting the inscription of sin.

Two trees are contrasted: the cursed shrub in the desert (v.6) and the blessed tree planted by water (v.8). The one who trusts in human strength is like a bush in the wasteland; the one who trusts in the LORD is like a tree whose roots reach the stream. The parallel with Psalm 1 is unmistakable — Jeremiah adapts the wisdom tradition for prophetic purposes.

refs.note: The blessed person "like a tree planted by streams of water" — the wisdom tradition that Jeremiah adapts for prophetic use.
God will write his law on hearts — the solution to the sin-engraved heart of 17:1.
Jesus teaches that evil comes "out of the heart" — the same anthropology of the deceitful heart.

notes.note: Sin engraved with an iron stylus and diamond point — the most durable writing implements. The inscription is permanent; human effort cannot erase it. Only divine intervention can overwrite it.
The curse on trusting in human strength: like a shrub in the desert, parched and barren. The Hebrew 'arâr (cursed) echoes the covenant curses of Deuteronomy.
The blessing on trusting the LORD: like a tree by water, green in drought, fruitful without anxiety. The contrast is absolute — no middle ground between trust and self-reliance.
The heart is "deceitful above all things and beyond cure." The Hebrew 'āqōb ("deceitful") puns on Jacob's name yet again — the heart is a "Jacob," a supplanter and deceiver.

Calvin calls this "the sum of true religion: to trust in God alone and renounce all confidence in creatures." The two trees represent two civilizations — one built on human resources, the other on divine provision.

Calvin uses this verse extensively in the Institutes to establish total depravity: "The heart is so corrupted that it deceives even the one who possesses it. Self-knowledge is impossible without divine illumination."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "iron stylus" ('ēṭ barzel) and "diamond point" (ṣippōren šāmîr) describe the most permanent form of inscription available — harder even than stone tablets.
The Hebrew 'āqōb is a rare adjective meaning "insidious/deceitful." Its paronomasia with Jacob ('yaʿaqōb) continues the wordplay from 9:4.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies a chiastic structure: sin on the heart (v.1) → two ways (vv.5-8) → the deceitful heart (v.9) → God searches hearts (v.10). The envelope structure places the heart at both beginning and end, framing the wisdom poem.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the two-ways poem as a direct challenge to Judah's foreign policy — trusting in Egypt or Babylon rather than in God. The "political" and "spiritual" readings are inseparable: geopolitical alliance is a form of idolatry.

hist.context: The "two ways" passage (vv.5-8) is one of the most quoted texts in Jeremiah — a wisdom poem contrasting trust in humanity with trust in God. The cursed person is like a desert bush; the blessed person like a tree by water. The famous declaration "The heart is deceitful above all things and beyond cure" (v.9) follows as the theological diagnosis: why do people choose the desert bush? Because the heart itself is diseased. Only God can search the heart (v.10).

heb.paragraph: God commands Sabbath observance at the city gates (v.21-22). The conditional promise is stark: keep the Sabbath and the Davidic dynasty endures; violate it and the city burns (vv.25-27). The Sabbath functions as a covenant marker — a weekly sign of Israel's exclusive relationship with God. Its violation signals the comprehensive covenant collapse that Jeremiah has been documenting.

refs.note: Nehemiah enforces Sabbath observance at the gates — the post-exilic community taking Jeremiah's warning seriously.
Jesus declares that "the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath" — recentering the institution on its purpose rather than its mechanics.

notes.note: Jeremiah's prayer: "Heal me, LORD, and I will be healed; save me and I will be saved." The passive voice acknowledges that salvation is entirely God's work.
The mockers ask: "Where is the word of the LORD? Let it come now!" — the ancient equivalent of "Where is this judgment you keep promising?" Delayed fulfillment provokes contempt.
The Sabbath command at the gates: do not carry loads through the gates on the Sabbath. The gates were the commercial centers — this is a command to stop business, trusting God for provision.
Calvin reads this as a model prayer of dependence: "Jeremiah does not ask God to assist his own healing efforts but to be the sole healer. True prayer renounces all self-sufficiency."

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The "gate of the people" (šaʿar bĕnê-hāʿām) may be the Benjamin Gate or another major entrance to Jerusalem. Gates were public gathering places where proclamations would reach maximum audience.
The geographical catalog — cities of Judah, Benjamin, the Shephelah, the hill country, the Negev — represents the full extent of Judean territory participating in restored temple worship.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom argues that the Sabbath oracle is not a legalistic intrusion but a covenant test case: if Judah cannot keep even this one commandment — the most visible sign of covenant loyalty — then the covenant itself is dead.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads the Sabbath demand as an anti-imperial act — stopping commerce means refusing to participate in the economic system that drives injustice. Sabbath is resistance.

hist.context: The Sabbath oracle (vv.19-27) may surprise readers accustomed to Jeremiah's emphasis on interior transformation — here he demands external observance. But the Sabbath is not mere ritual; it is the sign of the covenant (Exod 31:13-17), and its neglect is symptomatic of the deeper faithlessness Jeremiah has been diagnosing. The conditional structure (if/then) suggests that even at this late hour, obedience could avert judgment.

themes.note: Faith and Covenant both at 8 — the two-ways poem and Sabbath oracle both center on trust and covenant loyalty.

rows.label: vv.5-8
rows.text: Two Ways: Desert Bush vs. River Tree

lit.note: Chapter 17 moves from diagnosis (sin-engraved heart) through wisdom (two ways) to prescription (Sabbath observance). The confession (vv.14-18) provides the personal counterpoint.

ppl.name: Jeremiah
ppl.text: 5-8) is one of the most quoted texts in Jeremiah — a wisdom poem contrasting trust in humanity with trust in God…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Sin Engraved on the Heart

translations.text: That person will be like a bush in the wastelands; they will not see prosperity when it comes. They will dwell in the parched places of the desert, in a salt land where no one lives.
He is like a shrub in the desert, and shall not see any good come. He shall dwell in the parched places of the wilderness, in an uninhabited salt land.
This is what the Lord says: “Cursed is the one who trusts in man, who draws strength from mere flesh and whose heart turns away from the Lord.
Thus says the LORD: “Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from the LORD.

thread.text: The blessed person "like a tree planted by streams of water" — the wisdom tradition that Jeremiah adapts for prophetic use.
God will write his law on hearts — the solution to the sin-engraved heart of 17:1.
Jesus teaches that evil comes "out of the heart" — the same anthropology of the deceitful heart.
Nehemiah enforces Sabbath observance at the gates — the post-exilic community taking Jeremiah's warning seriously.
Jesus declares that "the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath" — recentering the institution on its purpose rather than its mechanics.

coaching.tip: 'Cursed is the one who trusts in man... Blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD' (vv. 5, 7). The tree-by-water image (v. 8) parallels Psalm 1 almost exactly. Whether Jeremiah quotes the Psalm or vice versa, the pairing reveals a wisdom tradition: the fundamental human choice is between self-reliance and God-reliance. Jeremiah locates this choice not in the temple but in the heart — 'the heart is deceitful above all things' (v. 9).

'Heal me, LORD, and I will be healed; save me and I will be saved' (v. 14). Jeremiah's prayer acknowledges that even his capacity to be healed depends on God's initiative. The double passive — healed/saved — eliminates human contribution. This is the prayer of someone who knows the heart's deceptiveness (v. 9) too well to trust his own repentance. He outsources even the wanting-to-be-saved to God.
